plan.description: The  Data Management Plan (DMP) for the project described in the file aims to ensure proper handling of the data produced during the research lifecycle. The DMP will follow the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) to manage, archive, and make the data publicly available. The plan will be established early in the project to standardize procedures across partners, ensuring harmonization of metadata standards, quality control, and data preservation. The DMP also includes policies for data sharing and updates as needed throughout the project..
plan.title: ROLE OF HABITAT DEGRADATION AND FOOD WEB STRUCTURE IN THE PERFORMANCE OF WETLANDS AS CARBON SINKS

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

reference.label: ROLE OF HABITAT DEGRADATION AND FOOD WEB STRUCTURE IN THE PERFORMANCE OF WETLANDS AS CARBON SINKS
Ministero dell'Università e della Ricerca (MUR)
reference.reference: Ministero dell'Università e della Ricerca (MUR) - BANDO 2022

reference.label: National Research Council of Italy– Institute for Agricultural and Forest Systems in Mediterranean

description.description: The context of the "Description of different data types" in the document refers to the management of data collected from various research activities, specifically focusing on wetlands and their ecosystem dynamics. The purpose is to ensure that all data types—such as carbon and nitrogen inputs, isotopic analysis, vegetation data, hydrological and satellite data, and food web structures—are clearly described, processed, and made interoperable for research and conservation purposes.
description.label: PRIN- Data Management Plan_Version_1

descriptionTemplate.label: Data Management Plan Template - University of Bologna
descriptionTemplate.description:
This template has been prepared by the Data Stewards of the University of Bologna to support researchers in drafting a Data Management Plan for their project. It consists of a form designed to describe any research output (data, software and/or non-digital objects) in turn and how it is managed throughout the project.

  • Section 1 asks about the research output creator(s) and allows to select which research output (data, software and/or non-digital objects) is being described. 
  • Section 2 is devoted to a description of the research output -- the questions here change according to the type of output selected in Section 1.
  • Section 3 asks you to describe the legal and ethical implications (if any) of handling the research output.
  • Section 4 asks you to describe how the research output is made Findable, Accessible, Interoperable and Reusable (FAIR).
  • Section 5 asks you to describe how data quality and integrity are ensured throughout the project.

fieldSet.title: Contact person(s)
fieldSet.description: The contact person has knowledge of how to access, troubleshoot, or otherwise address issues related to the research output.

fieldSet.title: Is the contact person also the research output creator?
fieldSet.description: Creators are usually identified among Work Package (WP) coordinators and research team leaders responsible for research output collection, organization, processing and management.

fieldSet.title: Research output creator(s) and contributor(s
fieldSet.description:
Creators are usually identified among Work Package (WP) coordinators and research team leaders responsible for research output collection, organization, processing and management.
If the contact person is the creator of the dataset, please insert information only about other contributors.

Possible other values for "Role" are defined as follows:
- Project member: a researcher indicated in the project grant agreement (GA);
- Researcher: an assistant to one of the authors who helped with research, but is not part of team indicated in the GA;
- Data collector: e.g., the survey conductor, or the experimenter;
- Other: please specify the role in brackets.

labelData.label: ALMA MATER STUDIORUM - Università di Bologna

fieldSet.title: Institution(s) involved
fieldSet.description: Please list the public or private institution(s) employing you and other creator(s), or the institution(s) on behalf of which the research was carried out. Please use the institutions' legal names (e.g., ALMA MATER STUDIORUM - Università di Bologna). If more than one institution is involved, enter each name in a separate field (it is possible to add up to 10).

fieldSet.title: What resources will be dedicated to data management and ensuring that data will be FAIR?
fieldSet.description:
By resources we mean financial or time costs that will be related to data management activities. 
As a reference, https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Work package and task number
fieldSet.description: In the context of your project, which WP and task has produced this research output? (Compile only if applicable.)

fieldSet.title: Which type of research output are you describing?
fieldSet.description:
You can choose between 3 typologies or research output: dataset, software or physical object. The questions that follow will change according to your selection.
Please bear in mind that "dataset" has a broad meaning here, and indicates a collection of data organized and managed with a specific aim in mind and reflecting a specific research activity.​
With "software" we indicate a set of instructions or programs used to operate computers and execute specific tasks (usually written in a specific programming language).
A "non-digital object" can be, for example, a medical device, a 3D-printed model, a physical primary source from a museum, library or archive.

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using an already existing dataset?
fieldSet.description: If the dataset you are using has been previously created (by you or someone else) as part of another research project, please select 'yes'. If the dataset is created anew as part of the current project, please select 'no'.

labelData.label: Describe why you are re-using an existing dataset and how it fits into your current project
Briefly demonstrate that you have taken into account any potential restrictions to data re-use

fieldSet.title: Re-using an existing dataset: why and how
fieldSet.description: Please describe below how the existing dataset fits into your current research project. Also, briefly demonstrate that you have taken into account any potential restrictions to data re-use (e.g., associated to copyright or other ethical aspects).

fieldSet.title: Creating a new dataset
fieldSet.description: Please describe below why you are not re-using an existing dataset but rather producing a new one as part of your current research project. Re-using exising data - if at all possible - is highly recommended as it avoids re-inventing the wheel. However, data on the phenomenon you are studying may not exist or may not be available to you (e.g. because of potential restrictions due to copyright or other ethical aspects).

section.description: By "dataset" we mean a collection of data organized/structured and managed with a specific aim in mind and reflecting a specific research activity.​
section.title: Identification of the dataset

fieldSet.title: Dataset name
fieldSet.description: If the dataset is re-used, insert here the original title. If the dataset is new, please remember to name  it according to consistent rules. We suggest to follow this convention: Project acronym. WPnumber. Short title consistent with the content of the data set. VersionNumber (if applicable)

fieldSet.title: Data types
fieldSet.description:
Please describe what types of data are included in the dataset. Data can be categorized in many different ways. For example:
  • numerical, textual, graphical, visual or tactile
  • born digital or digitized
  • quantitative o qualitative
  • raw, secondary, derived, cleaned, processed.
If there is more than one category, please enter each in a separate field (it is possible to add up to 10).

fieldSet.title: Data formats
fieldSet.description:
Please indicate the file formats used, possibly one per field (it is possible to add up to 10).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Size of data
fieldSet.description: Please select the order of magnitude of your dataset and add more precise information below, if available.

fieldSet.title: How is the data generated/collected
fieldSet.description:
Please explain in some detail the process of generating or collecting your data. Include a full citation of the data sources (if any) and describe how data provenance (information on the origin of data and how it is altered or transformed throughout its lifecycle) is tracked throughout the project.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Why is the data generated/collected
fieldSet.description: Please describe the purpose of data generation/collection in relation to the objectives of the project. Also include, if possible, the future uses that you envisage for your data, inside and outside the project's scope.

fieldSet.title: Dataset description
fieldSet.description: Please provide an abstract for your dataset, to be diplayed alongside it on, e.g., a data repository. It should give a clear idea of the content of the datatset and include (part or all of) the information already entered above.

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using already existing software?
fieldSet.description: If the software you are using has been previously created (by you or someone else) as part of another research project, please select 'yes'. If software is created anew as part of the current project, please select 'no'.

labelData.label: Describe how existing software fits into your current project
Briefly demonstrate that you have taken into account any potential restrictions to re-use

fieldSet.title: Re-using existing software: why and how
fieldSet.description: Please describe below how existing software fits into your current research project. Also, briefly demonstrate that you have taken into account any potential restrictions to re-use (e.g., associated to copyright or other ethical aspects).

fieldSet.title: Creating new software
fieldSet.description: Please describe below why you are not re-using existing software but rather producing it anew as part of your current research project. Re-using exising software - if at all possible - is highly recommended as it avoids re-inventing the wheel.

section.description: By "software" we mean a collection of instructions, data, or computer programs that are used to run machines and carry out particular activities.
section.title: Identification of software

fieldSet.title: Software name
fieldSet.description:
If existing software is re-used, insert here the original title. If software is new, please remember to name  it according to consistent rules. 
We suggest to follow naming conventions, such as snake_case, camelCase and so on. 
Name shall refer to project acronym and WPnumber (if applicable).
If suitable, semantic versioning should be considered too, for instance, using incrementing sequences or alphanumeric suffix where versions have affixed a dash plus some alphanumeric characters to indicate the status.

fieldSet.title: Type of software
fieldSet.description:
Please describe what type of software you are describing. For example: application software, system software etc.
For a complete reference taxonomy describing software types, check Ottawa Software Taxonomy:  http://www.site.uottawa.ca/eng/school/publications/techrep/2008

fieldSet.title: Software formats
fieldSet.description:
Please indicate the formats used, possibly one per field (it is possible to add up to 10), distinguishing, for example, between text formats (e.g. .txt) and programming language formats (e.g. .py, .js...).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Size of software
fieldSet.description: Please select the order of magnitude of your files and folders and add more precise information below, if available.

fieldSet.title: How is software created/used
fieldSet.description:
Please explain in some detail the process of creating or reusing software. Include a full citation of the sources (if any) and describe how provenance (information on how software is altered or transformed throughout its lifecycle) is tracked throughout the project.
If suitable, specify run-time environment specifications (programming language, runtime platform, operating system, other software requirements...)
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Why is the software created/used
fieldSet.description:
Please describe the purpose of software creation/use in relation to the objectives of the project. 
Also include, if possible, the future uses that you envisage for your software, inside and outside the project's scope.
For instace, specify the application category (e.g., "astronomical analysis") or target users (e.g., "employees of companies in the research and development sector").

fieldSet.title: Software description
fieldSet.description: Please provide an abstract for your software, to be diplayed alongside it on, e.g., a software repository. It should give a clear idea of the nature of the software and include (part or all of) the information already entered above.

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using an already existing object?
fieldSet.description: If the non-digital object you are using has been previously created (by you or someone else) as part of another research project, please select 'yes'. If the object is created anew as part of the current project, please select 'no'.

labelData.label: Describe why you are re-using an existing physical object and how it fits into your current project
Briefly demonstrate that you have taken into account any potential restrictions to re-use

fieldSet.title: Re-using an existing object: why and how
fieldSet.description: Please describe below why you are re-using an existing non-digital object and how it fits into your current research project. Also, briefly demonstrate that you have taken into account any potential restrictions to re-use (e.g., associated to copyright or other ethical aspects).

fieldSet.title: Creating a new object
fieldSet.description: Please describe below why you are not re-using an existing object but rather producing a new one as part of your current research project. Re-using what is out there - if at all possible - is highly recommended as it avoids re-inventing the wheel.

section.description: A "non-digital object" can be, for example, a medical device, a 3D-printed model, a physical primary source from a museum, library or archive.
section.title: Identification of non-digital object

fieldSet.title: Object name
fieldSet.description: If the non-digital object is re-used, insert here the original title. If it is new, please remember to name  it according to consistent rules.

fieldSet.title: Type of object
fieldSet.description: Please describe what type of non-digital object you are creating or re-using. You should include the technologies used, the object measurements and any other information that you deem relevant.

fieldSet.title: How is the object created/used
fieldSet.description:
Please explain in some detail the process of creationg or using the non-digital object. Include a full citation of the sources (if any) and describe how provenance (information on the origin of the object and how it is altered or transformed throughout its lifecycle) is tracked throughout the project.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Why is the object created/used
fieldSet.description: Please describe the purpose of the object creation/use in relation to the objectives of the project. Also include, if possible, the future uses that you envisage for it, inside and outside the project's scope.

fieldSet.title: Solutions adopted for handling personal data
fieldSet.description: Please describe briefly how you gathered the participants' informed consent and what strategies have been put in place to safeguard privacy once the data has been collected. Some examples include data anonymisation, pseudoanonymisation, data aggregation. See e.g., https://ec.europa.eu/justice/article-29/documentation/opinion-recommendation/files/2014/wp216_en.pdf for more details.

section.description: For a definition of personal data, please see: https://commission.europa.eu/law/law-topic/data-protection/reform/what-personal-data_en
section.title: Personal data

fieldSet.title: Are there any legal issues associated to your ouput? (e.g. IPR or valorization)
Legal issues: description and solutions
fieldSet.description:
Please describe the identified legal issues and briefly discuss:
- the process of obtaining approval from the ethics committee (if applicable)
- the strategies put in place to minimise or resolve any ethical issues once the data has been collected. These may include controlling access to data, without releasing them openly for society as a whole.

section.description:
This section describes legal issues that have an impact on your project and your research ouput.
Use it to describe if, as an example, IPR or valorization issues are present.
section.title: Legal aspects

fieldSet.title: Are there other ethical issues associated to your ouput?
Ethical issues: description and solutions
fieldSet.description:
Please describe the identified ethical issues and briefly discuss:
- the process of obtaining approval from the ethics committee (if applicable) 
- the strategies put in place to minimise or resolve any ethical issues once the data has been collected. These may include controlling access to data, without releasing them openly for society as a whole.

section.description: This section describes other ethical issues that have an impact on your project and your research ouput. These can incude the collection of confidential data, or data regarding security, the use of artificial intelligence (AI) or anything that could endanger the safety and well-being of human and animals.
section.title: Other ethical aspects

fieldSet.title: Selecting what to preserve
fieldSet.description:
Please indicate if anything needs to be retained or destroyed for contractual, legal, or regulatory purposes. Indicate if a selection on what to keep long-term is necessary and why (e.g., excessive size, easy replicability).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

selectData.label: Is your repository catalogued in re3data?
What type of repository is it?

fieldSet.title: Chosen repository/ies
fieldSet.description:
For dataset preservation strategy: Please complete the following table.
To do so, you need to choose at least one repository, to deposit you research output and ensure it is preserved after the end of the project. Repositories can be certified (e.g. CoreTrustSeal, ISO16363) or not, but should be commonly used and endorsed by international research communities in your discipline.
Please choose repository/ies that:
  • provide explicit information online about their policies and services (e.g., acquisition, access, security of content, long-term sustainability of service including funding etc.);
  • assign persistent unique identifiers to contents and ensure that contents are accompanied by enough machine-actionable and standardized metadata;
  • meet international and national criteria for security and have different levels of security depending on the sensitivity of what is being deposited.
For software preservation strategy: please complete the appropriate fields of the table (at least the "name" section, which is mandatory to proceed). If you need further space to provide more specific information and/or descriptions, please refer to question 4.1.3.

For non-digital objects preservation strategy: please complete at least the "name" section in the following table (which is mandatory to proceed). If you need further space to provide more specific information and/or descriptions, please refer to question 4.1.3.

fieldSet.title: Further information on long-term preservation strategy
fieldSet.description:
For software and non-digital objects preservation:
Please use this space to provide more specific information and/or descriptions regarding the long-term preservation strategy you are adopting.

section.description:
This section describes the policies adopted for preserving your research output long-term. 
Long-term preservation of datasets can be achieved  via a data repository, a digital infrastructure where data are deposited by researchers.
Data repositories might be chosen also for software preservation (e.g. via the Zenodo integration in GitHub), whilst dedicated services such as https://www.softwareheritage.org/ are developed specifically for this purpose.
Non-digital objects might require different strategies for preservation, depending on their nature (e.g. ancient books, biological samples, protptypes...).
section.title: Long-term preservation

fieldSet.title: Persistent identifier(s)
fieldSet.description:
Your research output must have at least one persistent identifier. PIDs are generally associated to a research output upon archival in a repository: make sure to choose a repository that automatically assigns a PID at (or soon after) upload. Types of identifiers include DOI, Handle, PURL, URL, URN, ISBN, check the type of persistent identification used by your repository service of choice.
If there are no persistent identifier(s) yet, just type “not yet available” and add it at a later stage.

fieldSet.title: Metadata
fieldSet.description:
Your research output must be accompanied by metadata. They are generally associated to a research output upon archival in a repository: make sure to choose a repository that uses recognised metadata standards.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Keywords
fieldSet.description:
Specify which keywords (if any) will be provided in the metadata to optimize discovery and re-use.
Please insert them separated by commas.

section.description: This section describes the steps taken to make your data findable online.
section.title: Findability

fieldSet.title: Is the research output openly accessible?
Controlled access
fieldSet.description: Please describe the reasons why your research output cannot be made openly accessible online.

fieldSet.title: Accessing the research output
fieldSet.description:
Please describe where and how your research output can be accessed (at least, in the repository/ies listed above). If it is not openly available, briefly describe the strategies adopted to control and grant access upon request.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Is metadata openly accessible?
fieldSet.description: Trusted repositories usually make metadata openly available online so that they can be found, for example, through an internet search. Please check the repository/ies you have chosen.

fieldSet.title: Accessing metadata
fieldSet.description:
Please describe the reasons why metadata cannot be made openly accessible online.
If you are an Horizon Europe grantee, metadata relative to your research output must be openly available under a CC 0, "No Rights Reserved" license, even if the output itself is not. Make sure that the repository/ies you have chosen comply with this requirement.

section.description: This section describes how your research output is made accessible online.
section.title: Accessibility

fieldSet.title: Methodologies
fieldSet.description:
Please describe the methodology/ies followed throughout the data lifecycle.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Vocabularies, taxonomies and other standards
fieldSet.description:
Please describe vocabularies, taxonomies and other standards used.
  • Generally, by 'vocabularies' or 'thesauri' we refer to those lexical resources composed of controlled terms to describe a set of elements, knowledge, data, theories belonging to a given scientific field or disciplinary domain.
  • With 'taxonomies', the elements are not only described by means of vocabularies of controlled and selected terms, but are also ordered in a system, generally hierarchical.
  • With 'ontologies', the types and thus the names of the relationships between the elements that make up a taxonomy or vocabulary are explicit. 
When collecting information, using at least a vocabulary can help can help to correctly identify the data and facilitate their reuse because they can be understood by others sharing a common vocabulary.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

section.description: This section describes the steps taken to make your research output interoperable.
section.title: Interoperability

fieldSet.title: Licensing
fieldSet.description:
Please state under which license your research output is released. It must be accompanied by a standard, machine-readable license. You can use tools such as https://chooser-beta.creativecommons.org/ (for datasets) or http://ufal.github.io/public-license-selector/ (for software) to choose a suitable license.
If you are a Horizon Europe grantee and opt for a license different from CC 0, "No Rights Reserved", or CC BY 4.0, "Attribution", you are required to explain why.
If you do not have detailed information yet, please select 'other' and provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Documentation
fieldSet.description:
Please specify which kind of documentation will be provided alongside your research output (e.g., codebooks, readme files). 
If your ouptut is a dataset or a physical object for which specific software is required, remember to reference and document it or, if possible, to include the software itself (e.g., if open source).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what documentation you plan to include.

section.description: This section describes the steps taken to make your research output re-usable for others.
section.title: Reusability

fieldSet.title: Measures implemented to ensure quality
fieldSet.description:
Explain how the consistency and quality of the research output will be checked and documented. This may include processes such as calibration, repeated samples or measurements, standardised data capture, data entry validation, peer review of data, or representation with controlled vocabularies (source: Science Europe).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

options.label: External Hard Drive (hard disk, USB key)
options.value: External Hard Drive (hard disk, USB key)

fieldSet.title: Storage
fieldSet.description:
Please specify the storage solution used during the active phases of your research. Explain how access is granted and tracked, especially in collaborative partnerships (source: Science Europe).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Backup
fieldSet.description:
Please describe your backup strategy and explain how files and folders will be recovered in the event of an incident (source: Science Europe).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

fieldSet.title: Additional security
fieldSet.description:
Consider the need for additional protection, particularly if your research output is sensitive - for example containing personal data, politically sensitive information, or trade secrets. Describe the main risks and how these will be managed (source: Science Europe).
If you do not have detailed information yet, please provide an outline of what you plan to do.

section.description: This section describes the steps taken to ensure the research output is not lost, damaged, or unlawfully accessed.
section.title: Security measures

field.textValue:
The methodologies followed throughout the data lifecycle, as outlined in our project, include standardized procedures based on the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable). These involve the collection, processing, ingestion, and preservation of data, using tools developed by LifeWatch to ensure that all research data is compliant with these principles.
Specifically:
1. Data Collection: Data is gathered in the field and laboratories using standardized techniques across various study areas. This involves collecting biological, chemical, and environmental data, such as carbon sequestration rates, vegetation coverage, and food web structures.
2. Data Processing: After collection, data is processed through calibrated models and analysis software to ensure quality control and consistency.
3. Ingestion and Preservation: Processed data is archived using the Data Schema of the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal, ensuring structured organization and accessibility.
4. Metadata: Metadata is associated with the data using the Ecological Metadata Language (EML 2.2.0), and a DOI (Digital Object Identifier) is assigned to each dataset.
For data management, a Data Management Plan (DMP) is developed early in the project, which includes harmonized standards and quality control procedures.

Keywords that will be provided in the metadata to optimize discovery and reuse of the dataset include:

Wetlands, carbon sequestration, food webs, biodiversity, habitat degradation, anthropogenic pressure, nitrogen inputs, climate change, ecosystem services, hydrology, vegetation cover, coastal lagoons, freshwater ponds, stable isotopes, organic matter decomposition, carbon cycling, nutrient inputs, ecosystem functioning, remote sensing, GIS, NDVI.
National Research Council Italy: ROMA, Lazio, IT
National Research Council of Italy (CNR) - Water Research Institute (IRSA)

1. Numerical Data: - Includes measurements such as carbon and nitrogen concentrations, CO2 emission rates, hydrological parameters (e.g., water temperature, salinity), and decomposition rates. - Type: Quantitative, raw, and processed.
2. Textual Data: - Descriptive information on site conditions, vegetation cover, anthropogenic pressure, and sampling methods. - Type: Qualitative, textual.

3. Graphical Data: - Geographic Information System (GIS) maps, such as drainage patterns and vegetation cover generated from Digital Elevation Models (DEM) and satellite imagery (NDVI). - Type: Visual, processed.
4. Derived Data: - Data on food web structures, derived from isotopic analysis (δ13C, δ15N) and reconstructed through modeling. - Type: Quantitative, derived.
5. Raw Data: - Field measurements of CO2 emissions, sediment samples, vegetation biomass, and isotopic values. - Type: Raw, quantitative.
6. Processed Data: - Cleaned and aggregated datasets of carbon and nitrogen inputs/outputs, vegetation indices, and hydrological variables, ready for analysis.

Dataset Abstract:
The dataset associated with the project "ROLE OF HABITAT DEGRADATION AND FOOD WEB STRUCTURE IN THE PERFORMANCE OF WETLANDS AS CARBON SINKS" provides comprehensive data on wetland ecosystems, focusing on how habitat degradation and alterations in food web structures influence their role as carbon sinks.
This dataset includes:
- Ecological and Environmental Data: Information on various wetland sites' physical, chemical, and biological conditions.
- Biomass and Species Diversity: Detailed records of species composition, biomass, and biodiversity across trophic levels, highlighting the effects of habitat degradation on these variables.
- Trophic Dynamics: Stable isotope analysis data used to reconstruct food webs, track nutrient transfers, and assess the impact of biodiversity changes on carbon cycling within wetlands.
- Hydrological Data: Models of water dynamics within wetland ecosystems, helping to evaluate the effects of water flow changes on carbon sequestration.
The dataset aims to offer insights into the interplay between wetland degradation, food web interactions, and their effectiveness as carbon sinks. It supports data-driven wetland restoration and management approaches, contributing to climate change mitigation efforts.
This dataset is intended for use by ecologists, environmental scientists, and policy-makers working on wetland conservation, climate action, and ecosystem sustainability.

The research output can be accessed through the LifeWatch ITA Data Portal and LifeWatch ERIC Metadata Catalogue, where all metadata and research data will be archived. If the data is not openly available, access can be granted upon request, and managed through controlled access mechanisms in compliance with the data management policies established. These strategies ensure that sensitive data is protected while still allowing authorized users to access the information through formal request processes.
If detailed information on access is not yet available, you can outline the plan to archive data in repositories such as LifeWatch ITA and indicate that a structured access request procedure will be implemented.

No major legal issues are expected concerning the output of this project. The dataset consists of ecological, hydrological, and biological data from wetland ecosystems and does not include personal or sensitive information. Therefore, the primary concern is ensuring compliance with open data and intellectual property rights under the chosen license.
-Ethics Committee Approval:
Ethics committee approval is not applicable since the dataset does not involve human subjects or personal data. However, in the case of any data involving fieldwork or protected wetland species, relevant permits and compliance with environmental regulations were obtained from the competent authorities prior to data collection.
-Strategies for Minimizing Ethical Issues:
a) Open Access and Licensing: All data will be shared openly under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC-BY 4.0) license, allowing for widespread use and adaptation while requiring proper attribution to the data creators. This ensures that the data can be freely accessed and utilized by the scientific community and the public.
b) Controlled Access (if required): In cases where specific data might involve sensitive environmental locations or species at risk (e.g., endangered wetland habitats), access to certain sensitive datasets may be restricted. In such instances, controlled access mechanisms will be put in place to protect vulnerable ecosystems while ensuring that the data remains available to authorized users, such as researchers and policy-makers.
c) Long-Term Preservation and Data Integrity: All datasets will be stored in a trusted repository that follows standard protocols for data security, versioning, and long-term preservation. The repository will ensure that the data is accessible and reusable while maintaining the integrity and authenticity of the original dataset.
These measures will ensure that legal and ethical concerns are addressed effectively while maintaining the openness and accessibility of the research data for societal and scientific benefit.

The data is generated and collected to support the core objectives of the project, which is to understand how habitat degradation and food web structure influence the carbon sink capacity of wetlands. Specifically, the data will help achieve the following key goals:
1. Assessing the Carbon Sink Capacity of Wetlands:
   - Data on carbon and nitrogen inputs/outputs, sediment composition, and organic matter decomposition are collected to quantify the ability of wetlands to store carbon and assess their role in mitigating climate change.
2. Investigating Food Web Structures:
   - The project uses isotopic analysis to map food webs in wetland ecosystems, focusing on how interactions between species influence carbon cycling, organic matter decomposition, and overall ecosystem productivity.
3. Understanding the Impact of Anthropogenic Pressure:
   - Data on water quality, land use patterns, and hydrological changes will help quantify the effects of human activities (e.g., pollution, agriculture) on wetland ecosystems and their functioning as carbon sinks.
4. Evaluating Habitat Conditions:
   - Remote sensing and NDVI data will be used to track changes in vegetation cover and productivity in response to environmental stressors, contributing to wetland conservation strategies.
### Future Uses of the Data:
- Inside the project scope:
  - The data will be used to model the impact of climate change and anthropogenic pressures on carbon sequestration and emissions in wetlands.
  - It will provide recommendations for wetland restoration and management, identifying appropriate targets and indicators for conservation efforts.
- Outside the project scope:
  - The dataset will be made publicly available for further research through the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal and the LifeWatch ERIC Metadata Catalogue, enabling its use in other studies related to ecosystem services, biodiversity, and climate change mitigation.
  - Policy makers, environmental agencies, and conservation groups can use the data to inform wetland management and contribute to international initiatives like the Ramsar Convention and the EU Water Framework Directive.

The data for this project is collected and generated through a combination of field experiments, laboratory analyses, and remote sensing technologies. Here's an outline of the process:
1. Field Data Collection:
   - Fieldwork will be conducted across three coastal lagoons and three freshwater ponds in Italy, focusing on various ecological parameters such as CO2 emissions, carbon and nitrogen inputs/outputs, water quality, and vegetation biomass.
   - Physicochemical parameters like temperature, salinity, pH, and conductivity are collected using multiparameter probes.
   - Sediment samples are taken to analyze organic matter and carbon storage, while aquatic vegetation, invertebrates, and fish are sampled for stable isotope analysis (δ13C, δ15N).
2. Remote Sensing and GIS Data:
   - Satellite imagery (from Landsat and Sentinel satellites) is used to compute Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), providing data on vegetation cover and productivity.
   - GIS mapping is conducted to track hydrological patterns, surface runoff, and anthropogenic pressures using Digital Elevation Models (DEM) and Corine Land Cover (CLC) maps.
3. Laboratory Experiments:
   - Isotopic analysis of collected samples (sediments, vegetation, and organisms) is conducted in the lab using mass spectrometry to determine carbon and nitrogen sources and food web structure.
   - Decomposition rates of organic matter are measured through controlled litterbag experiments in both field and laboratory settings.
4. Data Provenance:
   - The provenance of the data is carefully tracked by maintaining metadata for each dataset. Metadata follows the Ecological Metadata Language (EML) standard and includes information about data sources, collection methods, and any transformations or analyses applied to the raw data.
   - Every dataset will be assigned a unique identifier (DOI), and data transformations (e.g., processing or cleaning steps) will be documented to ensure transparency throughout the data lifecycle.
5. Data Citation:
   - Data generated will be made publicly available through the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal and LifeWatch ERIC Metadata Catalogue, with proper citations for satellite data sources (e.g., USGS Landsat, Sentinel).
This systematic process ensures that all data collected in the project adheres to the FAIR principles and remains traceable, reproducible, and transparent throughout its lifecycle.

1. Data to be Preserved:
Primary Research Data: All core datasets related to habitat degradation, food web structure, biomass, and carbon sequestration will be preserved in full. These include:
Ecological and environmental data on wetlands (e.g., biodiversity, species composition, carbon flow).
Hydrological models and data related to water dynamics within wetland systems.
Stable isotope data is used to reconstruct food webs and nutrient transfer within ecosystems.
Any associated field observations, measurements, and experimental data that contribute to the project’s outcomes.
2. Data to be Discarded:
Intermediary Data: Temporary or intermediary data, such as raw files that are used during the process of data cleaning or manipulation but have no added scientific value once refined, may not need to be preserved long-term.
Replicable Data: Data that can be easily replicated (e.g., certain computational or derived models) may not need to be preserved, especially if the process of generating it is well-documented and replicable using the retained data.
3. Legal and Regulatory Considerations:
There are no specific contractual or regulatory requirements mandating the destruction of data. However, data that falls under environmental regulations, particularly those about sensitive ecosystems, may need to be preserved with controlled access.
No sensitive personal data is included in the dataset, so there are no legal obligations to delete or anonymize data based on privacy concerns.
4. Plan for Selection:
In cases where the dataset size becomes excessive or where parts of the data are easily replicable, a selection process will be applied. Priority will be given to retaining:

Data that is unique and non-replicable, such as field measurements and experimental results.
Data that is highly valuable for future research, policy-making, or ecological restoration efforts.
Final decisions on data preservation will be made with input from researchers to ensure the retention of the most relevant and impactful information for future use.

The methodologies that will be followed for vocabularies, taxonomies, and other standards include the use of standardized vocabularies and controlled terms relevant to the environmental and ecological fields. For metadata, the project will use the Ecological Metadata Language (EML 2.2.0), which is a well-established standard within the LifeWatch infrastructure. Additionally, taxonomies related to species and ecological classifications will likely follow structured systems to ensure consistency and interoperability. This standardization supports the FAIR data principles and ensures that the research outputs are accessible and reusable. If more specific vocabularies or ontologies are not yet determined, the plan will indicate that recognized standards will be used, such as those from ecological and biological research communities.

The documentation will be provided alongside the research output and will include comprehensive metadata, associated with data using the Ecological Metadata Language (EML 2.2.0), and any necessary readme files or codebooks for interpreting datasets. If specific software is required to access or analyze the data, references to the software will be included, and where possible, open-source software will be provided. The data and models will be structured using the Data Schema of the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal, ensuring the output is well-documented for future use and reusability​

fieldSet.comment:
Below is an outline of the storage solution and access management:

1. Storage Solution:
a) Primary Storage:
  - Data will be stored on institutional servers managed by the University of Salento and its partners (Sapienza University of Rome, CNR-IRSA). These servers offer high storage capacity and ensure the data is securely backed up during the research phase.
  - Cloud storage may also be used to facilitate easy access and collaboration, especially for large datasets like GIS files or satellite imagery.
b) Backup:
  - Automatic regular backups will be scheduled on redundant servers to prevent data loss.
  - Data replication across **multiple geographic locations** will be implemented to ensure long-term preservation during the active research phase.

2. Access and Collaboration:
a) Access Control:
  - Access to the data will be role-based, with different levels of permissions granted to researchers, collaborators, and data managers.
  - Principal Investigators (PIs) and team leaders will have full administrative access, while other team members will have access limited to specific datasets or work packages.
  - Collaborators outside the core team will be granted access through secure logins, with read-only or edit privileges as needed, depending on their role.
b) Tracking Access:
  - Access logs will be maintained to track who accesses, modifies, or downloads the data. This ensures accountability and traceability of data use, especially in collaborative partnerships.
  - Collaborative tools such as shared folders and version control systems will be used to track changes made to datasets during active research. This ensures transparency and prevents overwriting of critical data.
c) Security:
  - Data access will be secured using multi-factor authentication (MFA) and encrypted connections to prevent unauthorized access during active phases of the project.
3. Future Plans:
- After the active research phase, the dataset will be transferred to long-term storage solutions like the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal, where it will be archived, indexed, and made available for reuse under controlled access conditions, ensuring compliance with the FAIR principles.

field.textValue:
A new dataset is being created as part of the current research project because existing datasets do not fully capture the specific phenomena this project is investigating. The project focuses on understanding the effects of habitat degradation and food web structure on the carbon sink capacity of wetlands, which requires unique experimental data from various locations (e.g., coastal lagoons and freshwater ponds) across different seasons.

Additionally:

Existing datasets may not provide data at the required spatial resolution or may lack specific measurements, such as stable isotope analysis of carbon and nitrogen or the detailed food web structures that this project aims to construct.
The project involves collecting new, site-specific data on carbon and nitrogen fluxes, vegetation cover, and hydrological changes in Italian wetlands, which might not be available in previous datasets.
Ethical or copyright restrictions may prevent the reuse of existing data, as some proprietary or unpublished datasets are not publicly accessible.
Thus, producing a new dataset is essential to meet the project’s objectives and ensure the accuracy and relevance of the research

In the project, resources dedicated to data management and ensuring that data will be FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) are well-defined. These include both financial and time-related costs to ensure compliance with best practices for research data management. Based on the information provided in the project document:

Financial Resources:

The project allocates specific budget items for data management activities, including the use of standardized tools for data collection, processing, ingestion, and preservation. This is in line with the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal, which ensures that data is managed according to the FAIR principles.
Resources are dedicated to metadata generation and management, ensuring data is properly described using the Ecological Metadata Language (EML) and assigned a DOI (Digital Object Identifier), which is essential for making data findable and accessible.
Time and Personnel Resources:

The project involves significant time investments from key personnel, including David Rossi and Maria Letizia Costantini, who contribute to data collection, stable isotope analysis, and food web reconstruction. The project tasks, such as data mining, meta-analysis, and isotopic analysis, are coordinated to ensure that all data remains accessible and interoperable.
The Data Management Plan (DMP) is developed early in the project (within the first 3-6 months) and continuously updated, reflecting the harmonized standards and quality control procedures.
WP4: Data Mining, Meta-Analysis, and Data Organization Tasks

Data mining from existing literature to calibrate field and lab experiments. Organize and manage all collected data following the FAIR principles. Structure data using the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal with associated metadata using Ecological Metadata Language (EML). Prepare a Data Management Plan (DMP) and maintain standardized procedures for data processing and sharing

fieldSet.comment:
The dataset for this project is expected to fall within the 1 GB to 20 GB range. This estimation includes:
- Numerical data (e.g., CSV, XLSX files): Likely small in size but abundant in quantity due to repeated measurements over time and across multiple wetland sites.
- GIS and graphical data (e.g., .shp, .geojson, JPG/PNG): These files could contribute more significantly to the size due to high-resolution satellite imagery and detailed maps.
- Raw and processed data from fieldwork and lab experiments (e.g., isotopic analysis, CO2 emissions, and decomposition rates), which may increase the overall size.

field.textValue:
For this project, while the primary data being collected is ecological and environmental (e.g., carbon and nitrogen inputs, food web structures, hydrology), there may still be a need for additional security measures to protect sensitive aspects of the research, such as the exact locations of protected wetlands or unpublished methodologies.
Potential Risks:
1. Data Integrity and Theft:
o Unauthorized access or malicious intent could lead to data theft, manipulation, or corruption, especially in a collaborative setting with multiple users accessing shared data.
2. Sensitive Ecological Data:
o The exact locations of sensitive or protected ecosystems (e.g., Ramsar sites, Natura 2000 areas) may need additional protection to prevent unwanted exploitation or harm to these areas.
3. Collaboration and Sharing Risks:
o In cases where external collaborators are granted access, there may be risks associated with data sharing over unsecured networks or unauthorized distribution of research output before publication.
Security Measures:
1. Data Encryption:
o All sensitive datasets, especially those involving geographical coordinates or specific environmental conditions of protected sites, will be encrypted both at rest and in transit using industry-standard encryption methods.
o Files will be shared through secure file transfer protocols (SFTP) to ensure data is not intercepted or altered during transmission.
2. Access Control:
o Access will be strictly limited to authorized personnel through role-based access controls (RBAC). Only designated researchers will have write/edit permissions, while other collaborators will be restricted to read-only access.
o Multi-factor authentication (MFA) will be enforced to add an extra layer of protection when accessing sensitive data.
3. Data Anonymization:
o If there is any risk of sensitive information exposure (e.g., exact locations of endangered species or vulnerable ecosystems), data anonymization techniques will be applied to remove specific identifying details from public datasets or shared files.
4. Regular Audits and Monitoring:
o Continuous monitoring of data access logs will be in place to track who accesses, modifies, or downloads data, ensuring transparency and accountability.
o Regular security audits will be conducted to identify vulnerabilities in the data management system and to ensure compliance with security protocols.
5. Incident Response Plan:
o In case of a security breach or data leak, a response plan will be in place, including immediate measures such as revoking access, investigating the breach, and restoring data from secure backups.

The metadata for the project’s research output will be generated and structured to ensure it adheres to recognized standards and facilitates data discovery, reuse, and long-term preservation.

Planned Metadata Strategy:
Metadata Standards:

The project will use the Ecological Metadata Language (EML) standard, a widely recognized metadata format for ecological and environmental datasets.
This ensures that the data will be findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable (FAIR) across various repositories and research platforms.
Content of Metadata:

The metadata will include the following key information:
Title: The name of the dataset, reflecting its content (e.g., "PRIN2022.WP1-5.Wetland_Ecosystem_Data").
Authors: The contributors and their affiliations, including roles such as data collectors, analysts, and PIs.
Description: A detailed abstract of the dataset, outlining the research context, objectives, data types, and collection methods.
Keywords/Tags: Specific keywords related to wetland ecosystems, carbon sequestration, food web structures, biodiversity, and climate change impacts.
Geospatial Information: Coordinates and descriptions of the locations of the wetlands studied (if applicable and not sensitive).
Temporal Coverage: The time period during which the data was collected (e.g., seasonal data across 2 years).
Provenance Information: Details on how the data was collected, processed, and transformed throughout its lifecycle.
Licensing and Access Information: Indicating if the data is open access or subject to specific licensing terms.
Data Format: File formats used (e.g., CSV, GIS, XLSX, etc.).
Repository Selection:

The dataset will be archived in the LifeWatch Italy Data Portal and LifeWatch ERIC Metadata Catalogue, both of which support EML and ensure compliance with international metadata standards.
These repositories will assign a unique DOI to the dataset, further supporting data tracking and citation.
Metadata Creation and Updates:

Metadata will be created early during the data collection process and continuously updated as the dataset evolves.
The project team will ensure that any changes in data processing or structure are reflected in the metadata, maintaining its accuracy and relevance throughout the project.
This comprehensive metadata will enhance the discoverability and usability of the dataset by other researchers and stakeholders, ensuring proper documentation and alignment with FAIR principles​
1. CSV (Comma-Separated Values): - For numerical data such as carbon and nitrogen concentrations, CO2 emission rates, and other field measurements. - Use: Raw and processed quantitative data.
2. TXT (Text Files): - For textual data, including site descriptions, sampling notes, and metadata. - Use: Qualitative information.
3. GIS Formats (e.g., .shp, .geojson): - For graphical data such as GIS maps of drainage patterns, vegetation cover, and NDVI. - Use: Visual, spatial data.
4. XLSX (Excel Spreadsheet): - For organizing and analyzing data, including data summaries, derived calculations, and meta-analysis. - Use: Quantitative and processed data.
5. JPG/PNG (Image Files): - For graphical representations of data, such as satellite images, maps, or graphs. - Use: Visual data.
6. PDF (Portable Document Format): - For reports, data summaries, and documentation of methodologies. - Use: Processed qualitative and graphical data.

To ensure the quality and consistency of the research output, several measures will be implemented throughout the project. These might include:
1. Standardized Data Collection: Standard protocols for data collection will be followed to ensure accuracy and reliability. Field and lab experiments will be conducted in the wetlands using consistent methodologies.
2. Calibration and Quality Control: Calibration of experiments, such as carbon measurements, will be applied. Regular calibration of instruments will ensure the accuracy of data collected on parameters like carbon uptake and release.
3. Repeated Sampling and Measurements: To check for consistency, repeated sampling in different seasons will be conducted. This approach will allow the team to cross-verify data and detect any inconsistencies in environmental conditions and data trends.
4. Data Entry Validation: Quality control procedures will be established to ensure that data entry is accurate and free from errors. This could involve double-checking data entry points and validating datasets before ingestion into the repository.
5. Peer Review: All data and metadata will be peer-reviewed before final submission to ensure adherence to the project’s data management plan.
6. Controlled Vocabularies: The use of controlled vocabularies (such as the Ecological Metadata Language (EML 2.2.0)) will ensure the standardization of terms and data descriptions, making the data more consistent and interoperable.

The reasons for not making the research output openly accessible online are primarily related to data management protocols involving sensitive environmental data and ensuring compliance with relevant policies. Controlled access may be needed to protect intellectual property, manage sensitive ecological data, or comply with legal frameworks governing environmental research, such as those ensuring the protection of wetland ecosystems or related policies mentioned in the document​

fieldSet.comment: The research output from your project will likely be licensed under Creative Commons licenses, such as CC BY 4.0 ("Attribution"), by the Horizon Europe guidelines. This license ensures that the data is shared with proper attribution to the authors, allowing for reuse and distribution.

field.textValue: PANGAEA (Data Publisher for Earth & Environmental Science)

The project will implement a robust backup strategy to ensure data safety and recovery in case of an incident. Here's the outline of the backup approach:
 Backup Strategy:
1. Regular Backups:
   - Automatic daily backups of all data will be scheduled on institutional servers, ensuring that no data is lost during active research.
   - A weekly full backup will be performed, with incremental backups happening daily to capture changes made to the datasets.
2. Redundant Storage Locations:
   - Backups will be stored in multiple locations, including:
     - Primary on-site servers at the University of Salento and partner institutions.
     - Off-site cloud storage to provide an additional layer of security and disaster recovery.
   - This redundancy ensures that in the event of server failure or physical damage, data can still be recovered from the remote backup.
3. Version Control:
   - A version control system will be used to manage different versions of datasets. This will allow recovery of specific versions of data in case of accidental overwriting or deletion.
 File and Folder Recovery:
- Recovery Procedures:
   - In case of data loss or corruption, files and folders can be restored from the latest backup using the backup management interface provided by the storage solution.
   - Recovery will be facilitated by the backup logs, which track all backup activities and identify the most recent stable version of any dataset.
- Time to Recovery:
   - Files can typically be restored within 24 to 48 hours of an incident, depending on the size of the dataset and the nature of the issue.
   - Immediate restoration can be performed from on-site backups for minor incidents, while off-site cloud backups will be used in the event of major incidents (e.g., hardware failure, cyberattacks).
 Testing and Monitoring:
   - The backup system will be regularly tested to ensure data can be recovered successfully.
   - Alerts and notifications will be set up to monitor backup success or failure, ensuring any issues are quickly addressed.
By implementing this comprehensive backup strategy, the project ensures data is fully protected and can be swiftly recovered in case of any incident.
